name: GitHub Actions CI for Python with Matrix Testing
description: Set up a complete GitHub Actions CI pipeline for Python projects with matrix testing across multiple Python versions, linting, type checking, and coverage.
prerequisites: A Python project in a GitHub repository
pytest and ruff installed as dev dependencies

steps.title: Create the CI workflow directory
steps.description: Create the directory structure that GitHub Actions requires for workflow files.

steps.title: Create the CI workflow file
steps.command:
cat > .github/workflows/ci.yml << 'EOF'
name: CI
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        python-version: ['3.11', '3.12', '3.13']
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: pip install pytest pytest-cov ruff mypy
      - run: ruff check .
      - run: ruff format --check .
      - run: mypy src/ --ignore-missing-imports
      - run: pytest --cov=src --cov-report=xml tests/
EOF
steps.description: Create a CI workflow that tests against Python 3.11, 3.12, and 3.13. It runs linting, formatting checks, type checking, and tests with coverage.
steps.tip: The matrix strategy runs all Python versions in parallel, so the total CI time equals the slowest single version, not the sum.

steps.title: Validate the workflow syntax locally
steps.description: Verify that the workflow file is valid YAML before committing. Syntax errors in workflow files are only caught after pushing to GitHub.
steps.warning: This checks YAML syntax only, not GitHub Actions schema. For full validation, use 'actionlint' if installed.

steps.title: Commit and push the workflow
steps.command: git add .github/workflows/ci.yml && git commit -m 'ci: add Python CI pipeline' && git push
steps.description: Commit the workflow file and push to GitHub. The CI pipeline will trigger automatically on the next push or pull request to main.

steps.title: Check the workflow run status
steps.description: Use the GitHub CLI to check the status of recent workflow runs. This shows whether your CI pipeline passed or failed.
steps.tip: Use 'gh run view <run-id> --log-failed' to see the logs for a failed run without opening a browser.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# GitHub Actions CI setup for Python
set -e

mkdir -p .github/workflows

cat > .github/workflows/ci.yml << 'WORKFLOW'
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
      - run: pip install ruff
      - run: ruff check .
      - run: ruff format --check .

  typecheck:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: pip install mypy
      - run: mypy src/ --ignore-missing-imports

  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false
      matrix:
        python-version: ['3.11', '3.12', '3.13']
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
          cache: pip
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: pip install pytest pytest-cov
      - run: pytest --cov=src --cov-report=xml --cov-fail-under=80 tests/
      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: matrix.python-version == '3.12'
        with:
          name: coverage-report
          path: coverage.xml
WORKFLOW

echo "CI workflow created at .github/workflows/ci.yml"
echo "Commit and push to activate the pipeline."

faq.question: How do I cache pip dependencies in GitHub Actions?
faq.answer: Use the 'cache: pip' option in actions/setup-python@v5. It automatically caches the pip download cache based on your requirements.txt hash, reducing install times on subsequent runs.

faq.question: What does fail-fast: false do in the matrix strategy?
faq.answer: By default, if one matrix job fails, GitHub cancels all other running jobs. Setting fail-fast: false lets all versions complete, so you can see the full picture of which Python versions pass and which fail.

faq.question: How do I add a status badge to my README?
faq.answer: Add this markdown to your README: ![CI](https://github.com/OWNER/REPO/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg). Replace OWNER and REPO with your GitHub username and repository name.
